subject: Product Management
title: Twitter's Identity Crisis
description: A quick, practical guide to Twitter's Identity Crisis.

hook.text: Twitter knew what it was: a real-time public conversation platform. The problem was it couldn't decide what to become next.

buildup.text: Twitter's core product barely changed for a decade: 140-character posts, a reverse-chronological timeline, and a follow model.

discovery.text: User growth stalled at 330 million while Instagram and TikTok surged past it. Advertisers followed the growth.

twist.text: Twitter launched and killed features constantly: Moments, Fleets, Spaces, Super Follows — none stuck or solved the growth problem.

climax.text: Twitter's inability to evolve its core product left it vulnerable to acquisition. It became an example of product stagnation.

punchline.text: Knowing who you are isn't enough. Products must evolve or be replaced.

quiz.question: What was Twitter's core product failure?
options: Inability to evolve the core product — constantly launching and killing features without a clear direction
Twitter was too expensive for advertisers
